title: Erythroderma
oneLiner: Widespread erythema and scaling (>90% body surface area) – a red, hot, sick patient who needs urgent dermatology and metabolic support.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette**  
A 65-year-old man with a 10-year history of chronic plaque psoriasis suddenly develops diffuse, fiery-red skin covering his entire trunk, limbs, and face over 3 days. He feels systemically unwell: fever (38.5°C), tachycardia, and shaking chills. On examination, his skin is warm to touch, exfoliating in large sheets (desquamation), with pitting oedema in his lower legs. His palms and soles are thickened and scaly. He reports recent cessation of his topical steroid and vitamin D analogue combination, and has just completed a course of amoxicillin for a chest infection.

**Dissection of pattern clues**
- **Diffuse redness >90% BSA** – hallmark of erythroderma; loss of skin barrier function
- **Fever, tachycardia, chills** – systemic inflammation; skin cannot regulate temperature; fluid loss → hypovolaemia
- **Desquamation (sheets of skin)** – rapid epidermal turnover; hallmark of exfoliative dermatitis
- **Lower limb oedema** – hypoalbuminaemia from protein loss through damaged skin; also heart failure if fluid overload
- **Palmar/plantar thickening** – clue for underlying psoriasis (pustular psoriasis variant)
- **Recent steroid withdrawal** – triggers rebound in psoriasis, or topical steroid withdrawal syndrome
- **Recent antibiotic (amoxicillin)** – drug hypersensitivity reaction (DRESS syndrome mimic)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you**
1. **Subacute onset** – develops over weeks, mimicking atopic dermatitis. Patient feels less toxic, but still has >90% erythema. Often due to underlying cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (Sézary syndrome) – look for pruritus, lymphadenopathy, and atypical lymphocytes on blood film.
2. **Neonatal erythroderma** – baby presents with red, scaly, sick appearance. Differential includes Netherton syndrome, Omenn syndrome, or severe atopic dermatitis. Check for failure to thrive, recurrent infections, and family history.
3. **Drug-induced without exfoliation** – e.g., allopurinol, carbamazepine, phenytoin. Skin is red and tender but not yet scaling. May have mucosal involvement (Stevens-Johnson overlap). Temperature >39°C and facial oedema signal DRESS.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Erythroderma is defined as diffuse erythema and scaling involving more than 90% of the body surface area. It is a **clinical syndrome**, not a single disease – a final common pathway of many triggers (psoriasis, eczema, drugs, lymphoma). It is a medical emergency because the skin loses its barrier, thermoregulation, and fluid/electrolyte homeostasis, leading to high-output cardiac failure, hypothermia, and sepsis.

**Why it matters clinically**
- **Frequency**: Uncommon but critical. Incidence ~1-2 per 100,000 / year. Most common causes in adults: psoriasis (25%), eczematous dermatitis (25%), drugs (20%), idiopathic (15%), lymphoma (10%).
- **Stakes**: Mortality up to 10-20% if unrecognised or mismanaged. Hypothermia, hypoalbuminaemia, and superinfection are the big killers.
- **Pitfalls**: Mistaking for simple widespread dermatitis, delaying biopsy for lymphoma, giving topical steroids without treating underlying cause, or missing drug withdrawal as trigger.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
The skin is the largest organ. In erythroderma, a massive inflammatory cascade (cytokines: TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-17) drives keratinocyte hyperproliferation and vasodilation. The normal epidermal turnover time of 28 days collapses to 4-5 days, causing premature desquamation. This explains every clinical sign:

- **Erythema** → Vasodilation from cytokines (prostaglandins, histamine). Blood flow may increase 10-fold, giving the bright red colour.
- **Warm skin** → Increased cutaneous blood flow; also fever from IL-1/IL-6 acting on hypothalamus.
- **Desquamation** → Accelerated epidermal transit; horny layer sheds in large sheets. Loss of stratum corneum breaks the barrier.
- **Hypothermia** → Evaporative heat loss through damaged skin (up to 2-3 L/day of water loss). Paradoxical: warm to touch but core temperature can drop.
- **Tachycardia, increased cardiac output** → High-output state from massive vasodilation and fluid loss; can precipitate high-output heart failure.
- **Oedema** → Hypoalbuminaemia (protein lost through shed skin) reduces oncotic pressure. Also increased capillary leak from inflammation.
- **Itching** → Histamine release; but also IL-31 and other pruritogens from activated T cells.
- **Lymphadenopathy** → Dermatopathic lymphadenitis from draining inflamed skin; but if firm, rubbery → ? lymphoma.
- **Hypocalcaemia** (rare but classic exam pearl) → Loss of calcium in shed skin; also vitamin D deficiency due to failure of cutaneous synthesis.

**Connection to pattern recognition**  
The patient with tachycardia, fever, and oedema is in **early decompensation**. The drug history (antibiotic, steroid withdrawal) is the key trigger in this case. The palmar-plantar thickening is a psoriasis clue → erythrodermic psoriasis.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Cause Category | Specific Triggers | Stem Clue |
|---------------|------------------|-----------|
| **Pre-existing dermatosis (most common)** | Psoriasis (especially pustular) – rebound after steroid withdrawal / infection / stress | “Stopped his topical steroids – now red all over” |
| | Atopic dermatitis / eczema | Long history of itchy, flexural eczema that worsened |
| | Seborrhoeic dermatitis | Scalp, face, and central chest red, greasy scales |
| | Pityriasis rubra pilaris (PRP) | Orange-red colour, islands of sparing, palmoplantar keratoderma |
| **Drugs** | Allopurinol, carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, sulfonamides, dapsone, hydroxychloroquine, vancomycin | Onset 2-6 weeks after starting drug; often with facial oedema, fever, eosinophilia |
| **Idiopathic** | No clear cause (15-20%) – but must rule out lymphoma | “After extensive workup, no trigger found” |
| **Malignancy** | Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (Sézary syndrome, mycosis fungoides); rarely solid tumours (lung, colon) – paraneoplastic | Intense pruritus, lymphadenopathy, circulating Sézary cells, “red man syndrome” |
| **Infections** | HIV (as immune reconstruction or acute seroconversion); staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS – in children); toxic shock syndrome | Fever, hypotension, mucosal involvement, desquamation later |
| **Autoimmune / other** | Dermatomyositis (juvenile or adult); GVHD; pemphigus foliaceus; Kawasaki disease (children) | Periorbital heliotrope, Gottron’s papules, or mucosal bullae |

**Most common overall cause** – Pre-existing psoriasis (especially if severe, or pustular variant). But in hospital inpatients, **drug reaction** is the most frequent trigger, so always check the medication chart.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic approach** – history (onset, drugs, past skin disease), full skin exam (mucous membranes, nails, hair, lymph nodes), then directed tests.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Full blood count** | Eosinophilia → drug reaction; lymphocytosis / atypical cells → CTCL; neutrophilia → infection / pustular psoriasis | Screen for drug reaction, lymphoma, infection | Eosinophils >1.5 x 10⁹/L strongly suggests DRESS. Sezary cells >20% of lymphocytes → CTCL. |
| **LFTs, U&Es, CRP, albumin** | Hypoalbuminaemia (<25 g/L) = severity marker; elevated LFTs → drug hepatitis / DRESS; CRP high in infection; AKI from dehydration | Assess metabolic derangement | Albumin <20 g/L → higher mortality; start aggressive nutritional support |
| **Blood cultures + skin swabs** | Bacterial growth → superinfection (Staph aureus, Pseudomonas) | Fever + erythroderma = rule out sepsis | Positive cultures guide antibiotics |
| **Chest X-ray** | Hilar lymphadenopathy → lymphoma; interstitial infiltrates → DRESS / drug pneumonitis | Detect pulmonary involvement | Also assess for heart failure |
| **Skin biopsy** (punch from erythematous area) | Histology: spongiosis (eczema), acanthosis (psoriasis), Pautrier microabscesses (CTCL) | Confirm underlying dermatosis; rule out lymphoma | Single most useful test if cause unclear. Repeat if first biopsy non-diagnostic. |
| **Peripheral blood flow cytometry** | Abnormal T-cell population (CD4+CD7-, CD4+CD26-) → Sézary syndrome | Suspected CTCL | Diagnostic for erythrodermic CTCL |
| **Toxicology screen** | Drug metabolites (e.g. phenytoin, sulfonamide) | Suspected drug-induced | Confirms exposure |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms**  
- **Skin biopsy showing Pautrier microabscesses** → Confirms cutaneous T-cell lymphoma.  
- **Eosinophilia + fever + transaminitis + facial oedema** → DRESS syndrome (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms). No single test confirms, but RegiSCAR criteria help.

**What rules out**  
- **Normal albumin and normal skin biopsy** suggests early, possibly drug-induced or idiopathic – treat conservatively.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour)** – The ABCDE of the skin crisis.

1. **Airway / Breathing** – high-flow oxygen if hypoxic (consider pulmonary oedema from high-output failure).
2. **Circulation** – IV fluids (0.9% saline) 30 mL/kg if hypotensive. Central line for CVP monitoring. Start cardiac monitoring.
3. **Disability** – check core temperature (rectal). If <35°C, active warming (Bair Hugger). If >38.5°C, paracetamol 1g IV.
4. **Exposure** – cover patient with sterile sheets; avoid wet dressings until haemodynamically stable.
5. **Stop all non-essential drugs** – especially suspect ones (antibiotics, anticonvulsants, allopurinol). If psoriasis, **do not stop systemic steroids suddenly** – taper.
6. **First-line topical** – emollients (50:50 white soft paraffin/liquid paraffin) applied gently every 2-4 hours. Avoid potent topical steroids until cause known.

**Ward (24-72 hours)** – Supportive care and treat underlying cause.

- **Fluids and electrolytes** – daily weight, U&E, albumin. Replace losses: insensible water loss up to 3 L/day. Correct hypoalbuminaemia with 20% human albumin if <20 g/L (50-100 mL IV q8h).
- **Nutrition** – high calorific feed (2-3 g/kg/day protein) via NG if unable to eat. Erythroderma patients are catabolic.
- **Temperature control** – if hypothermic, continue warming. If febrile, cooling blankets.
- **Infection prophylaxis** – skin swabs and blood cultures q48h. If S. aureus isolated, start flucloxacillin 500 mg IV QDS for 7 days.
- **Specific treatment based on cause**:
  - **Psoriasis** – Methotrexate 10-25 mg PO weekly (with folic acid 5 mg daily, except day of MTX) OR acitretin 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO. Consider TNF-α inhibitor (infliximab) if severe.
  - **Atopic eczema** – Ciclosporin 2.5-5 mg/kg/day PO (check BP, renal function). Prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day with slow taper over 4-6 weeks.
  - **Drug reaction** – Stop drug. Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day PO/IV with taper over 4-6 weeks. If DRESS, consider IVIG 2 g/kg over 2 days.
  - **CTCL (Sézary)** – Refer to haematology/oncology. Photopheresis, interferon, or brentuximab.
- **Skin care** – bland emollients, oatmeal baths for pruritus (avoid soap). If pruritus severe, antihistamines: cetirizine 10 mg PO BD or hydroxyzine 25 mg PO QDS. Avoid topical steroids on widespread skin (risk of adrenal suppression).

**Common pitfalls – what NOT to do**
- **Don’t give potent topical steroids** (e.g., clobetasol) over >30% BSA – risk of systemic absorption and adrenal suppression.
- **Don’t start systemic steroids without a dermatology consult** – especially if psoriasis (can trigger pustular flare).
- **Don’t forget to stop the culprit drug** – the most common mistake.
- **Don’t ignore temperature instability** – both hypo- and hyperthermia are lethal.
- **Don’t assume idiopathic** – always repeat skin biopsy if first is non-diagnostic.

**Long-term**
- Outpatient follow-up by dermatology. If cause identified (e.g., psoriasis), optimise long-term disease control (biologics, phototherapy). If drug-induced, document allergy in all records. If CTCL, lifelong monitoring.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
Top mimics for erythroderma (generalised redness), not all are >90% BSA but can be confused initially.

| Feature | Erythroderma | Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (SSSS) | DRESS Syndrome | Kawasaki Disease (adult: rare, but classic mimic) |
|---------|--------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Burning, tight | Diffuse tenderness | Painful skin; arthralgia | Myalgia, abdominal pain |
| **Exam** | >90% erythema + desquamation; Nikolsky sign negative (unless drug-induced with TEN overlap) | Positive Nikolsky (blistering with lateral pressure); superficial bullae; perioral crusting | Facial oedema, diffuse erythema, lymphadenopathy, palmoplantar desquamation later | Conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, desquamation of fingers/toes in subacute phase |
| **Mucosa** | Usually spared (unless drug-induced/TEN overlap) | Mucosa usually spared | Can have mild oropharyngeal erythema | Cervical lymphadenopathy (>1.5 cm), red cracked lips |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia ± signs of high-output failure | Sinus tachycardia | Non-specific; may see myocarditis | Coronary artery ectasia/aneurysm (echocardiogram) |
| **Labs** | Hypoalbuminaemia, eosinophilia (if drug), elevated LFTs, anaemia | Leukocytosis, elevated CRP; skin biopsy: granular layer split | Eosinophilia >10%, transaminitis, renal impairment | Elevated ESR/CRP, thrombocytosis in subacute phase; hypoalbuminaemia |
| **Key differentiator** | Skin biopsy shows acanthosis/spongiosis/lymphoma; Nail changes (psoriasis: oil drop, pitting) | Age <5 years; recent staph infection; histology: bulla at granular layer | RegiSCAR score ≥5; recent drug exposure 2-6 weeks ago | Fever >5 days + ≥4 of 5 criteria (conjunctivitis, rash, cervical lymphadenopathy, oral changes, extremity changes); echocardiogram abnormal |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
1. **High-output cardiac failure** – presents with persistent tachycardia, oedema, pulmonary crackles, elevated JVP. Key sign: bounding pulses. Do: echo, CVP monitoring, diuretics (furosemide 40 mg IV), fluid restriction.
2. **Hypothermia** – insidious onset. Core temp <35°C, bradycardia, confusion. Do: warm IV fluids, Bair Hugger, stop cooling. Avoid vasopressors.
3. **Sepsis / cellulitis** – usually Staph aureus or Pseudomonas. Signs: spreading erythema outside original area, purulent discharge, new fever, hypotension. Do: cultures, empiric antibiotics (flucloxacillin + gentamicin if severe).
4. **Hypoalbuminaemic malnutrition** – progressive oedema, ascites, poor wound healing. Do: NG feeding, albumin infusion if <20 g/L.
5. **Pulmonary embolism / DVT** – from immobility + hypercoagulable state. Do: prophylactic LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated.
6. **Death** – overall mortality 10-20%. Late presentation: multi-organ failure from hypothermia + sepsis + cardiac failure.

**What happens if untreated** – The patient becomes progressively hypothermic, develops severe oedema, then becomes hypotensive (from both high-output failure and sepsis), then ARDS, renal failure, coma, and death within 1-2 weeks.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**

1. **Causes of Erythroderma – “PRIDE”**
   - **P**soriasis (pustular)
   - **R**eaction to drugs
   - **I**diopathic
   - **D**ermatitis (atopic, seborrhoeic, eczematous)
   - **E**rythrodermic lymphoma (CTCL)

2. **Danger signs in erythroderma – “HOT SKIN”**
   - **H**ypothermia (core <35°C)
   - **O**edema (generalised)
   - **T**achycardia (>120 bpm)
   - **S**evere hypoalbuminaemia (<20 g/L)
   - **K**idney injury (AKI)
   - **I**nfection (superimposed)
   - **N**utrition failure (catabolic)

**Clinical pearls**
- In any red, hot patient, measure the core temperature – it is often low despite the warm skin.
- Palmo-plantar thickening suggests psoriasis or PRP.
- Lymphadenopathy + severe pruritus = suspect CTCL until proven otherwise.
- Drug-induced erythroderma often has facial oedema and eosinophilia – draw blood for flow cytometry early.
- If the patient is on a systemic steroid for psoriasis and becomes erythrodermic, DO NOT STOP the steroid abruptly – taper over weeks to avoid pustular flare.
- Topical emollients are the safest initial treatment regardless of cause.
- Every erythrodermic patient should have a skin biopsy – and if non-diagnostic, repeat after 2 weeks.

**Common exam mistakes**
- Confusing erythroderma with toxic shock syndrome – check for mucosal involvement and hypotension.
- Ordering potent topical steroids before excluding drug reaction – if the patient has a drug reaction, steroids may worsen it.
- Failing to check for underlying lymphoma – always send flow cytometry for Sézary cells.
- Not recognising high-output failure as the cause of tachycardia – treat with diuretics, not just beta-blockers.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Trigger | Action | Threshold |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| **Core temperature <35°C** | Call senior; start active warming; move to HDU/ICU | Hypothermia unresponsive to passive rewarming |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially respiratory rate >24, O2 sat <90%, systolic BP <90) | Escalate to medical registrar / critical care outreach | NEWS2 triggers |
| **Lactate >4 mmol/L** | Sepsis screen; IV fluids; ICU consult | Lactate check in any unwell erythroderma |
| **GCS < 13** | Septic shock or hepatic encephalopathy? Call ICU | Immediate ABCE |
| **Systolic BP <90 despite 2L crystalloid** | Consider vasopressors (noradrenaline); ICU admission | Fluid-refractory shock |
| **Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours** | Acute kidney injury – nephrology review | AKI stage 2+ |
| **New arrhythmia (AF, VT)** | Cardiac failure – call cardiology and ICU | High-output failure with arrhythmia |
| **Worsening respiratory distress with CXR whiteout** | ARDS – ICU for non-invasive ventilation | CPAP/BiPAP trial or intubation |

**Always involve dermatology early (same day) for skin biopsy and management plan. Involve intensive care early if haemodynamic instability or hypothermia refractory to simple measures.**

rawMarkdown:
## Erythroderma
**One-liner:** Widespread erythema and scaling (>90% body surface area) – a red, hot, sick patient who needs urgent dermatology and metabolic support.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette**  
A 65-year-old man with a 10-year history of chronic plaque psoriasis suddenly develops diffuse, fiery-red skin covering his entire trunk, limbs, and face over 3 days. He feels systemically unwell: fever (38.5°C), tachycardia, and shaking chills. On examination, his skin is warm to touch, exfoliating in large sheets (desquamation), with pitting oedema in his lower legs. His palms and soles are thickened and scaly. He reports recent cessation of his topical steroid and vitamin D analogue combination, and has just completed a course of amoxicillin for a chest infection.

**Dissection of pattern clues**
- **Diffuse redness >90% BSA** – hallmark of erythroderma; loss of skin barrier function
- **Fever, tachycardia, chills** – systemic inflammation; skin cannot regulate temperature; fluid loss → hypovolaemia
- **Desquamation (sheets of skin)** – rapid epidermal turnover; hallmark of exfoliative dermatitis
- **Lower limb oedema** – hypoalbuminaemia from protein loss through damaged skin; also heart failure if fluid overload
- **Palmar/plantar thickening** – clue for underlying psoriasis (pustular psoriasis variant)
- **Recent steroid withdrawal** – triggers rebound in psoriasis, or topical steroid withdrawal syndrome
- **Recent antibiotic (amoxicillin)** – drug hypersensitivity reaction (DRESS syndrome mimic)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you**
1. **Subacute onset** – develops over weeks, mimicking atopic dermatitis. Patient feels less toxic, but still has >90% erythema. Often due to underlying cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (Sézary syndrome) – look for pruritus, lymphadenopathy, and atypical lymphocytes on blood film.
2. **Neonatal erythroderma** – baby presents with red, scaly, sick appearance. Differential includes Netherton syndrome, Omenn syndrome, or severe atopic dermatitis. Check for failure to thrive, recurrent infections, and family history.
3. **Drug-induced without exfoliation** – e.g., allopurinol, carbamazepine, phenytoin. Skin is red and tender but not yet scaling. May have mucosal involvement (Stevens-Johnson overlap). Temperature >39°C and facial oedema signal DRESS.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
Erythroderma is defined as diffuse erythema and scaling involving more than 90% of the body surface area. It is a **clinical syndrome**, not a single disease – a final common pathway of many triggers (psoriasis, eczema, drugs, lymphoma). It is a medical emergency because the skin loses its barrier, thermoregulation, and fluid/electrolyte homeostasis, leading to high-output cardiac failure, hypothermia, and sepsis.

**Why it matters clinically**
- **Frequency**: Uncommon but critical. Incidence ~1-2 per 100,000 / year. Most common causes in adults: psoriasis (25%), eczematous dermatitis (25%), drugs (20%), idiopathic (15%), lymphoma (10%).
- **Stakes**: Mortality up to 10-20% if unrecognised or mismanaged. Hypothermia, hypoalbuminaemia, and superinfection are the big killers.
- **Pitfalls**: Mistaking for simple widespread dermatitis, delaying biopsy for lymphoma, giving topical steroids without treating underlying cause, or missing drug withdrawal as trigger.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
The skin is the largest organ. In erythroderma, a massive inflammatory cascade (cytokines: TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-17) drives keratinocyte hyperproliferation and vasodilation. The normal epidermal turnover time of 28 days collapses to 4-5 days, causing premature desquamation. This explains every clinical sign:

- **Erythema** → Vasodilation from cytokines (prostaglandins, histamine). Blood flow may increase 10-fold, giving the bright red colour.
- **Warm skin** → Increased cutaneous blood flow; also fever from IL-1/IL-6 acting on hypothalamus.
- **Desquamation** → Accelerated epidermal transit; horny layer sheds in large sheets. Loss of stratum corneum breaks the barrier.
- **Hypothermia** → Evaporative heat loss through damaged skin (up to 2-3 L/day of water loss). Paradoxical: warm to touch but core temperature can drop.
- **Tachycardia, increased cardiac output** → High-output state from massive vasodilation and fluid loss; can precipitate high-output heart failure.
- **Oedema** → Hypoalbuminaemia (protein lost through shed skin) reduces oncotic pressure. Also increased capillary leak from inflammation.
- **Itching** → Histamine release; but also IL-31 and other pruritogens from activated T cells.
- **Lymphadenopathy** → Dermatopathic lymphadenitis from draining inflamed skin; but if firm, rubbery → ? lymphoma.
- **Hypocalcaemia** (rare but classic exam pearl) → Loss of calcium in shed skin; also vitamin D deficiency due to failure of cutaneous synthesis.

**Connection to pattern recognition**  
The patient with tachycardia, fever, and oedema is in **early decompensation**. The drug history (antibiotic, steroid withdrawal) is the key trigger in this case. The palmar-plantar thickening is a psoriasis clue → erythrodermic psoriasis.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Cause Category | Specific Triggers | Stem Clue |
|---------------|------------------|-----------|
| **Pre-existing dermatosis (most common)** | Psoriasis (especially pustular) – rebound after steroid withdrawal / infection / stress | “Stopped his topical steroids – now red all over” |
| | Atopic dermatitis / eczema | Long history of itchy, flexural eczema that worsened |
| | Seborrhoeic dermatitis | Scalp, face, and central chest red, greasy scales |
| | Pityriasis rubra pilaris (PRP) | Orange-red colour, islands of sparing, palmoplantar keratoderma |
| **Drugs** | Allopurinol, carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, sulfonamides, dapsone, hydroxychloroquine, vancomycin | Onset 2-6 weeks after starting drug; often with facial oedema, fever, eosinophilia |
| **Idiopathic** | No clear cause (15-20%) – but must rule out lymphoma | “After extensive workup, no trigger found” |
| **Malignancy** | Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (Sézary syndrome, mycosis fungoides); rarely solid tumours (lung, colon) – paraneoplastic | Intense pruritus, lymphadenopathy, circulating Sézary cells, “red man syndrome” |
| **Infections** | HIV (as immune reconstruction or acute seroconversion); staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS – in children); toxic shock syndrome | Fever, hypotension, mucosal involvement, desquamation later |
| **Autoimmune / other** | Dermatomyositis (juvenile or adult); GVHD; pemphigus foliaceus; Kawasaki disease (children) | Periorbital heliotrope, Gottron’s papules, or mucosal bullae |

**Most common overall cause** – Pre-existing psoriasis (especially if severe, or pustular variant). But in hospital inpatients, **drug reaction** is the most frequent trigger, so always check the medication chart.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic approach** – history (onset, drugs, past skin disease), full skin exam (mucous membranes, nails, hair, lymph nodes), then directed tests.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Full blood count** | Eosinophilia → drug reaction; lymphocytosis / atypical cells → CTCL; neutrophilia → infection / pustular psoriasis | Screen for drug reaction, lymphoma, infection | Eosinophils >1.5 x 10⁹/L strongly suggests DRESS. Sezary cells >20% of lymphocytes → CTCL. |
| **LFTs, U&Es, CRP, albumin** | Hypoalbuminaemia (<25 g/L) = severity marker; elevated LFTs → drug hepatitis / DRESS; CRP high in infection; AKI from dehydration | Assess metabolic derangement | Albumin <20 g/L → higher mortality; start aggressive nutritional support |
| **Blood cultures + skin swabs** | Bacterial growth → superinfection (Staph aureus, Pseudomonas) | Fever + erythroderma = rule out sepsis | Positive cultures guide antibiotics |
| **Chest X-ray** | Hilar lymphadenopathy → lymphoma; interstitial infiltrates → DRESS / drug pneumonitis | Detect pulmonary involvement | Also assess for heart failure |
| **Skin biopsy** (punch from erythematous area) | Histology: spongiosis (eczema), acanthosis (psoriasis), Pautrier microabscesses (CTCL) | Confirm underlying dermatosis; rule out lymphoma | Single most useful test if cause unclear. Repeat if first biopsy non-diagnostic. |
| **Peripheral blood flow cytometry** | Abnormal T-cell population (CD4+CD7-, CD4+CD26-) → Sézary syndrome | Suspected CTCL | Diagnostic for erythrodermic CTCL |
| **Toxicology screen** | Drug metabolites (e.g. phenytoin, sulfonamide) | Suspected drug-induced | Confirms exposure |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms**  
- **Skin biopsy showing Pautrier microabscesses** → Confirms cutaneous T-cell lymphoma.  
- **Eosinophilia + fever + transaminitis + facial oedema** → DRESS syndrome (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms). No single test confirms, but RegiSCAR criteria help.

**What rules out**  
- **Normal albumin and normal skin biopsy** suggests early, possibly drug-induced or idiopathic – treat conservatively.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour)** – The ABCDE of the skin crisis.

1. **Airway / Breathing** – high-flow oxygen if hypoxic (consider pulmonary oedema from high-output failure).
2. **Circulation** – IV fluids (0.9% saline) 30 mL/kg if hypotensive. Central line for CVP monitoring. Start cardiac monitoring.
3. **Disability** – check core temperature (rectal). If <35°C, active warming (Bair Hugger). If >38.5°C, paracetamol 1g IV.
4. **Exposure** – cover patient with sterile sheets; avoid wet dressings until haemodynamically stable.
5. **Stop all non-essential drugs** – especially suspect ones (antibiotics, anticonvulsants, allopurinol). If psoriasis, **do not stop systemic steroids suddenly** – taper.
6. **First-line topical** – emollients (50:50 white soft paraffin/liquid paraffin) applied gently every 2-4 hours. Avoid potent topical steroids until cause known.

**Ward (24-72 hours)** – Supportive care and treat underlying cause.

- **Fluids and electrolytes** – daily weight, U&E, albumin. Replace losses: insensible water loss up to 3 L/day. Correct hypoalbuminaemia with 20% human albumin if <20 g/L (50-100 mL IV q8h).
- **Nutrition** – high calorific feed (2-3 g/kg/day protein) via NG if unable to eat. Erythroderma patients are catabolic.
- **Temperature control** – if hypothermic, continue warming. If febrile, cooling blankets.
- **Infection prophylaxis** – skin swabs and blood cultures q48h. If S. aureus isolated, start flucloxacillin 500 mg IV QDS for 7 days.
- **Specific treatment based on cause**:
  - **Psoriasis** – Methotrexate 10-25 mg PO weekly (with folic acid 5 mg daily, except day of MTX) OR acitretin 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO. Consider TNF-α inhibitor (infliximab) if severe.
  - **Atopic eczema** – Ciclosporin 2.5-5 mg/kg/day PO (check BP, renal function). Prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day with slow taper over 4-6 weeks.
  - **Drug reaction** – Stop drug. Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day PO/IV with taper over 4-6 weeks. If DRESS, consider IVIG 2 g/kg over 2 days.
  - **CTCL (Sézary)** – Refer to haematology/oncology. Photopheresis, interferon, or brentuximab.
- **Skin care** – bland emollients, oatmeal baths for pruritus (avoid soap). If pruritus severe, antihistamines: cetirizine 10 mg PO BD or hydroxyzine 25 mg PO QDS. Avoid topical steroids on widespread skin (risk of adrenal suppression).

**Common pitfalls – what NOT to do**
- **Don’t give potent topical steroids** (e.g., clobetasol) over >30% BSA – risk of systemic absorption and adrenal suppression.
- **Don’t start systemic steroids without a dermatology consult** – especially if psoriasis (can trigger pustular flare).
- **Don’t forget to stop the culprit drug** – the most common mistake.
- **Don’t ignore temperature instability** – both hypo- and hyperthermia are lethal.
- **Don’t assume idiopathic** – always repeat skin biopsy if first is non-diagnostic.

**Long-term**
- Outpatient follow-up by dermatology. If cause identified (e.g., psoriasis), optimise long-term disease control (biologics, phototherapy). If drug-induced, document allergy in all records. If CTCL, lifelong monitoring.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
Top mimics for erythroderma (generalised redness), not all are >90% BSA but can be confused initially.

| Feature | Erythroderma | Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (SSSS) | DRESS Syndrome | Kawasaki Disease (adult: rare, but classic mimic) |
|---------|--------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Burning, tight | Diffuse tenderness | Painful skin; arthralgia | Myalgia, abdominal pain |
| **Exam** | >90% erythema + desquamation; Nikolsky sign negative (unless drug-induced with TEN overlap) | Positive Nikolsky (blistering with lateral pressure); superficial bullae; perioral crusting | Facial oedema, diffuse erythema, lymphadenopathy, palmoplantar desquamation later | Conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, desquamation of fingers/toes in subacute phase |
| **Mucosa** | Usually spared (unless drug-induced/TEN overlap) | Mucosa usually spared | Can have mild oropharyngeal erythema | Cervical lymphadenopathy (>1.5 cm), red cracked lips |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia ± signs of high-output failure | Sinus tachycardia | Non-specific; may see myocarditis | Coronary artery ectasia/aneurysm (echocardiogram) |
| **Labs** | Hypoalbuminaemia, eosinophilia (if drug), elevated LFTs, anaemia | Leukocytosis, elevated CRP; skin biopsy: granular layer split | Eosinophilia >10%, transaminitis, renal impairment | Elevated ESR/CRP, thrombocytosis in subacute phase; hypoalbuminaemia |
| **Key differentiator** | Skin biopsy shows acanthosis/spongiosis/lymphoma; Nail changes (psoriasis: oil drop, pitting) | Age <5 years; recent staph infection; histology: bulla at granular layer | RegiSCAR score ≥5; recent drug exposure 2-6 weeks ago | Fever >5 days + ≥4 of 5 criteria (conjunctivitis, rash, cervical lymphadenopathy, oral changes, extremity changes); echocardiogram abnormal |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
1. **High-output cardiac failure** – presents with persistent tachycardia, oedema, pulmonary crackles, elevated JVP. Key sign: bounding pulses. Do: echo, CVP monitoring, diuretics (furosemide 40 mg IV), fluid restriction.
2. **Hypothermia** – insidious onset. Core temp <35°C, bradycardia, confusion. Do: warm IV fluids, Bair Hugger, stop cooling. Avoid vasopressors.
3. **Sepsis / cellulitis** – usually Staph aureus or Pseudomonas. Signs: spreading erythema outside original area, purulent discharge, new fever, hypotension. Do: cultures, empiric antibiotics (flucloxacillin + gentamicin if severe).
4. **Hypoalbuminaemic malnutrition** – progressive oedema, ascites, poor wound healing. Do: NG feeding, albumin infusion if <20 g/L.
5. **Pulmonary embolism / DVT** – from immobility + hypercoagulable state. Do: prophylactic LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated.
6. **Death** – overall mortality 10-20%. Late presentation: multi-organ failure from hypothermia + sepsis + cardiac failure.

**What happens if untreated** – The patient becomes progressively hypothermic, develops severe oedema, then becomes hypotensive (from both high-output failure and sepsis), then ARDS, renal failure, coma, and death within 1-2 weeks.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics**

1. **Causes of Erythroderma – “PRIDE”**
   - **P**soriasis (pustular)
   - **R**eaction to drugs
   - **I**diopathic
   - **D**ermatitis (atopic, seborrhoeic, eczematous)
   - **E**rythrodermic lymphoma (CTCL)

2. **Danger signs in erythroderma – “HOT SKIN”**
   - **H**ypothermia (core <35°C)
   - **O**edema (generalised)
   - **T**achycardia (>120 bpm)
   - **S**evere hypoalbuminaemia (<20 g/L)
   - **K**idney injury (AKI)
   - **I**nfection (superimposed)
   - **N**utrition failure (catabolic)

**Clinical pearls**
- In any red, hot patient, measure the core temperature – it is often low despite the warm skin.
- Palmo-plantar thickening suggests psoriasis or PRP.
- Lymphadenopathy + severe pruritus = suspect CTCL until proven otherwise.
- Drug-induced erythroderma often has facial oedema and eosinophilia – draw blood for flow cytometry early.
- If the patient is on a systemic steroid for psoriasis and becomes erythrodermic, DO NOT STOP the steroid abruptly – taper over weeks to avoid pustular flare.
- Topical emollients are the safest initial treatment regardless of cause.
- Every erythrodermic patient should have a skin biopsy – and if non-diagnostic, repeat after 2 weeks.

**Common exam mistakes**
- Confusing erythroderma with toxic shock syndrome – check for mucosal involvement and hypotension.
- Ordering potent topical steroids before excluding drug reaction – if the patient has a drug reaction, steroids may worsen it.
- Failing to check for underlying lymphoma – always send flow cytometry for Sézary cells.
- Not recognising high-output failure as the cause of tachycardia – treat with diuretics, not just beta-blockers.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
| Trigger | Action | Threshold |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| **Core temperature <35°C** | Call senior; start active warming; move to HDU/ICU | Hypothermia unresponsive to passive rewarming |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially respiratory rate >24, O2 sat <90%, systolic BP <90) | Escalate to medical registrar / critical care outreach | NEWS2 triggers |
| **Lactate >4 mmol/L** | Sepsis screen; IV fluids; ICU consult | Lactate check in any unwell erythroderma |
| **GCS < 13** | Septic shock or hepatic encephalopathy? Call ICU | Immediate ABCE |
| **Systolic BP <90 despite 2L crystalloid** | Consider vasopressors (noradrenaline); ICU admission | Fluid-refractory shock |
| **Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours** | Acute kidney injury – nephrology review | AKI stage 2+ |
| **New arrhythmia (AF, VT)** | Cardiac failure – call cardiology and ICU | High-output failure with arrhythmia |
| **Worsening respiratory distress with CXR whiteout** | ARDS – ICU for non-invasive ventilation | CPAP/BiPAP trial or intubation |

**Always involve dermatology early (same day) for skin biopsy and management plan. Involve intensive care early if haemodynamic instability or hypothermia refractory to simple measures.**
